benchmark: allowlist selectivity crossover -- in-kernel pushdown vs naive post-filter (Phase C / C-2)
claim_under_test: a selective allowlist makes turbovec.knn(..., allowed) CHEAPER, not more expensive, because search_with_mask skips 32-vector blocks with zero allowed slots before any LUT work
path_measured: turbovec::IdMapIndex::search_with_allowlist (byte-identical kernel path to src/knn.rs::run_search; knn() always builds a FLAT IdMapIndex, no IVF cells, so this bench is faithful to the shipped allowlist path)
baseline: naive post-filter: unfiltered search at k*oversample candidates, then drop ids not in the allowed set, keep top-k (the pgvector-style post-filter a user would otherwise write in SQL)

host.name: floki (local NixOS worktree /tmp/pgtv-c)
host.simd: avx2 (compiled with -C target-cpu=native; AVX2 path exercised)

software.turbovec_crate: fork rev d72c29c (search.rs BLOCKS_SKIPPED_BY_MASK counter present)

notes: Absolute numbers are host-dependent. The SHAPE is the point: allowlist p50 decreases monotonically as the allowlist tightens, while the naive post-filter is flat (it always scans the whole corpus at k*oversample regardless of selectivity).
blocks_skipped_total is the cumulative turbovec::search::BLOCKS_SKIPPED_BY_MASK counter over all `iters` queries at that fraction -- direct evidence the in-kernel block short-circuit fires.
Crossover (allowlist beats naive post-filter) lands at roughly 7-10% selectivity on this host. Below that the allowlist wins and the win grows as the filter tightens.

At the full-mask end (fraction 1.0) the allowlist path is ~2.6x SLOWER than the unfiltered baseline: building+checking a 300k-bit mask with zero blocks skipped is pure overhead. Use an allowlist only when it is selective; for non-selective filters prefer a partial index or plain ANN. See docs/FILTERING.md.
